coredata.dc:title: Beyond photosynthesis: Engineering self-healing photocatalytic systems for sustainability

coredata.dc:description: Self-repairing photocatalysts represent a groundbreaking advancement in photocatalysis, addressing key challenges such as catalyst degradation, material fatigue, and efficiency loss across diverse applications. Inspired by natural photosynthesis, these systems incorporate self-healing mechanisms that restore functionality and extend operational lifespans, even under harsh environmental conditions. This review delves into the fundamental principles, innovative design strategies, and emerging trends in self-repairing photocatalysts, emphasizing their transformative potential in sustainable fuel production, environmental remediation, and carbon fixation. Key topics include molecular-level self-repair mechanisms, surface regeneration, bio-inspired adaptive interfaces, and multi-step repair strategies. Advanced characterization techniques, such as in situ monitoring and time-resolved spectroscopy, are highlighted for their role in optimizing self-healing processes. The synergy between photocatalytic activity and self-repair capabilities is explored through applications such as water splitting, CO₂ reduction, and wastewater treatment, showcasing systems that effectively mitigate degradation.Bio-inspired approaches, including peptide-based self-assembly and metal-organic frameworks, demonstrate exceptional stability and efficiency in photocatalytic systems. Additionally, cutting-edge molecular repair mechanisms, such as artificial enzyme cascades and dynamic covalent chemistry, are examined for their potential to enhance system longevity and performance. Advancements in real-time electron microscopy and AI-assisted degradation monitoring are also reviewed, offering insights into atomic-level repair processes and enabling predictive maintenance to sustain long-term functionality. The review further highlights the implementation of self-repairing photocatalysts in industrial-scale applications, including solar fuel production, CO₂ reduction, and wastewater treatment. Challenges related to scalability, cost-effectiveness, and long-term stability are addressed, with proposed solutions to overcome these barriers. Future research directions emphasize quantum dot-based self-repair systems, bio-hybrid catalysts, and AI-driven adaptive responses, paving the way for commercially viable, self-maintaining photocatalytic systems. These innovations hold immense promise for advancing sustainable energy production, environmental remediation, and carbon fixation, offering critical solutions to global sustainability challenges.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the Biological PSII Repair Process, Illustrating the Key Steps: Photoinhibition and D1 Degradation (i), Disassembly of PSII (ii), Insertion of Newly Synthesized D1 (iii), and Light-Induced Reassembly of the OEC (iv). [10].

The green, orange, and gray colors represent stable, unstable, and inactivity-inducing ligand environments for the metal catalyst M, respectively. Blue denotes a material that can anchor M, while reagents A are shown in red (S for substrate; P for product) [10]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Side views (a) and top views (b) of the atomic structures for Mo oxo-thio complexes adsorbed on a WSe₂ monolayer with a W-vacancy, highlighting SSe interactions and accessible disulfide groups in dimer Mo complexes. From left to right: adsorption configurations of MoS₄, MoS₃O, Mo₂S₁₂, and Mo₂S₆O₂ radicals. Color code: Yellow = Sulfur, Red = Oxygen, Violet = Molybdenum, Green = Selenium, Gray = Tungsten. (c) Free energy diagram for hydrogen evolution at equilibrium: (c1) ΔG_ads(H) (Gibbs free energy of hydrogen adsorption) showing values close to the optimum of the volcano curve for free-standing (Mo₂S₁₂)2− and (Mo₂S₆O₂)2− complexes; (c2) ΔG_ads(H) values for WSe₂ monolayers alone, including defect-free, W-vacancy, and Se-vacancy configurations; (c3) ΔG_ads(H) values for various adsorbed Mo complexes on defect-free, W-vacancy, and Se-vacancy WSe₂ monolayers. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Formation and optical properties of carbon dot liposomes (CDsomes). (A) Schematic illustration of the pyrolysis of triolein, followed by hydrolysis and self-assembly to form CDsomes. (B) TEM image showing the morphology of CDsomes. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDsomes. (D) Bright-field and fluorescence images of CDsomes captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope with various excitation wavelength filters. (E) Fluorescence images of CDsomes demonstrating photoswitching properties under sequential excitation at 360–390 nm and 525–545 nm, recorded using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Scale bar in (D): 10 μm.

(a) Measured contact angles (Θ) and (b) calculated total apparent surface free energies (γₛᵗ°ᵗ) for Ag-TiO₂/pPFDAc hybrid layers as a function of Ag-TiO₂ content. Dashed lines serve as visual guides. The inset in (a) shows the advancing (θ ₐdᵥ) and receding (θ ᵣₑc) contact angles measured on a pPFDAc thin film using the Wilhelmy plate method. [86].
Schematic illustration of the self-healing mechanism in PT-4 hydrogel, driven by hydrogen bonding [111].
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic hydrogenation process converting 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) using PT-4 hydrogel.

A carbon-fixing material can be achieved through a compartmentalized system that integrates CO₂ adsorption and catalytic processes. This system includes: (i) a high-capacity CO₂ adsorbent, (ii) a photocatalyst for reducing CO₂ to reactive monomers, and (iii) polymerization conditions that may employ a separate catalyst or initiator to extend the carbon backbone. The system continuously grows and self-repairs using sunlight and atmospheric CO₂ as inputs [115].

The complete chemical pathway for a carbon-fixing poly(oxymethylene) (POM) involves three sequential steps: (1) photocatalytic reduction of CO₂ to formaldehyde, (2) conversion of formaldehyde to 1,3,5-trioxane, and (3) polymerization of trioxane to POM. The proposed reaction network integrates three functional units: the photocatalytic unit (green), the trimerization unit (blue), and the polymerization unit (orange). To optimize this process, chemical kinetics and reaction engineering must address two key bottlenecks: (i) improving photocatalytic activity to accelerate the slow CO₂ reduction reaction (k₁ and k₂), and (ii) enhancing trioxane formation and polymerization (k₁₀) to enable faster POM growth [115]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The preparation process of epoxy resin matrix samples containing microcapsules [116].
Illustration of the working mechanism of the self-healing system [139].
The fabrication process for photocatalytic capsules, illustrating the steps involved in their preparation [139].
Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for the self-cleaning F-TiO₂/APDMS film, showcasing its durable superhydrophobicity and photocatalytic capability [140].
Schematic illustration of the self-healing mechanism of the F-TiO₂/APDMS film [140].

(A)Dyed water droplets on various treated (left) and untreated (right) substrates, demonstrating the water-repellent properties of the superhydrophobic (SH) coating. The treated substrates exhibit spherical droplets, highlighting their effective water resistance, while the untreated substrates show spreading droplets, indicating a lack of water repellency. (B) A dyed water droplet rolling on the treated surface when immersed in oil, illustrating the retained super hydrophobicity of the coating even in oil environments(a). A dyed water droplet spreading on the untreated glass slide when immersed in oil, emphasizing the absence of water-repellent properties(b). Dirt removal test at the oil-film-air interface, showcasing the self-cleaning ability of the SH coating in oil-contaminated conditions (c and d). Schematic illustration of SH-coated glass slides placed in water or oil, demonstrating their buoyancy in water and submersion in oil due to differences in surface interactions(e) [164].
Surface morphology of W-NUV, W-UV, Ti3-NUV, and Ti3-UV samples: (a) macroscopic images and (b) 3D surface morphology.

Summary of key studies on photocatalytic self-healing materials, highlighting their main findings and limitations. The table includes details on the development of photocatalytic self-healing coatings, films, and composites, as well as their potential applications and challenges in practical use.

row.entry: Main Findings
Limitations
Reference

- Developed photocatalytic self-healing mortar using capsules filled with repair materials activated by sunlight.
- Sunlight effectively heals cracks by filling them with insoluble calcium salts, NaHCO3, C6H8O7, and Ca(OH)2.
- The healed mortar showed improved waterproof properties.

- The depth of sunlight penetration into cracks was not quantified.
- More advanced photocatalysts and waterproof materials for capsules need to be explored.
- Impact of bubbles generated during healing and long-term effects of unreacted Ca(OH)2 and rain exposure remain unclear.

- Developed F-TiO2/APDMS films with superhydrophobicity (contact angle = 158°) and high photocatalytic capability (98 % dye degradation).
- Films exhibited self-repairing superhydrophobicity after UV damage, even after five cycles.

- Prolonged photocatalytic degradation reduces superhydrophobicity over time.
- Performance can be damaged by external factors such as chemicals or physical forces.

- Successfully synthesized CaCO3/TiO2 hybrid microcapsules with self-repair and self-cleaning properties.
- Microcapsules exhibited high thermal stability and effective photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue.
- Self-repair rate of 71.3 % for micro-cracks at 7.0 wt%.

- No explicit limitations were stated.
- Further research may be needed for large-scale industrial applications and optimizing the balance between healing and photocatalytic activity.

- Created a self-healing poly-acrylic acid/TiO2 hybrid hydrogel using UV light.
- Hydrogel's self-healing is attributed to hydrogen bonds between TiO2 and carboxylic groups.
- The hydrogel maintains photocatalytic performance in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol.

- Collection and reuse of photocatalyst after use may be a practical challenge.
- Focus has primarily been on photocatalytic performance, without consideration for recyclability or reusability.

- Introduced a theoretical framework for carbon fixing materials that convert CO2 into high-strength polymers.
- Developed a kinetic model for CO2 photoreduction to formaldehyde.
- The framework sets upper performance limits for photocatalysts in carbon fixation.

- Plant-based systems exhibit inefficiency and short lifetimes.
- Current photocatalytic CO2 reduction systems have low efficiency, poor selectivity, and competing reactions.
- Further experimental data is required to enhance photocatalyst performance.

- Cobalt oxide films exhibit self-healing within a specific voltage range (1.00–1.30 V).
- Addition of cobalt ions to the electrolyte enables self-repair.
- Structural changes in the films led to a decrease in turnover frequency over time.

- Self-healing and self-repair mechanisms are complex and require further research.
- Stability and performance depend on the applied voltage, limiting the self-repair function.
- Catalytic performance degrades over time due to structural changes in the material.

- Created superhydrophobic surfaces using TiO2 and octadecyl isocyanate (ODI).
- Coatings exhibited excellent self-cleaning, self-healing, and photocatalytic properties, maintaining performance even after UV exposure.
- Deposition method allows application on various substrates.
- No specific limitations mentioned, but challenges may include ensuring long-term stability under harsh conditions.

- Ag-TiO2 particles incorporated into polymer films to tune wetting properties from superhydrophobic to superhydrophilic.
- Films exhibited photocatalytic activity for ethanol degradation.
- Superhydrophobic films successfully photodegraded apolar SUDAN IV dye but not aqueous Methylene Blue dye.

- Rough nanoscopic surfaces may reduce long-term effectiveness.
- Photocatalytic activity may decrease with repeated use.
- Excessive Ag-TiO2 content interferes with PDMS polymerization.

- Mo thio- and oxo-thio-complex films served as HER catalysts on 2D WSe2 photoelectrodes.
- Achieved photocurrents up to 2.0 mA/cm2 at −0.2 V vs NHE, indicating efficient hydrogen evolution.
- Mo monomers and complexes optimized photocatalytic performance.

- Further research is needed to improve catalytic properties by doping Mo complexes with other metals or rare earth elements.
- 2D materials may be suited for tandem cell applications, requiring further exploration.

head.ce:title: Beyond photosynthesis: Engineering self-healing photocatalytic systems for sustainability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-repairing photocatalysts represent a groundbreaking advancement in photocatalysis, addressing key challenges such as catalyst degradation, material fatigue, and efficiency loss across diverse applications. Inspired by natural photosynthesis, these systems incorporate self-healing mechanisms that restore functionality and extend operational lifespans, even under harsh environmental conditions. This review delves into the fundamental principles, innovative design strategies, and emerging trends in self-repairing photocatalysts, emphasizing their transformative potential in sustainable fuel production, environmental remediation, and carbon fixation.

Key topics include molecular-level self-repair mechanisms, surface regeneration, bio-inspired adaptive interfaces, and multi-step repair strategies. Advanced characterization techniques, such as in situ monitoring and time-resolved spectroscopy, are highlighted for their role in optimizing self-healing processes. The synergy between photocatalytic activity and self-repair capabilities is explored through applications such as water splitting, CO₂ reduction, and wastewater treatment, showcasing systems that effectively mitigate degradation.Bio-inspired approaches, including peptide-based self-assembly and metal-organic frameworks, demonstrate exceptional stability and efficiency in photocatalytic systems. Additionally, cutting-edge molecular repair mechanisms, such as artificial enzyme cascades and dynamic covalent chemistry, are examined for their potential to enhance system longevity and performance. Advancements in real-time electron microscopy and AI-assisted degradation monitoring are also reviewed, offering insights into atomic-level repair processes and enabling predictive maintenance to sustain long-term functionality. The review further highlights the implementation of self-repairing photocatalysts in industrial-scale applications, including solar fuel production, CO₂ reduction, and wastewater treatment. Challenges related to scalability, cost-effectiveness, and long-term stability are addressed, with proposed solutions to overcome these barriers. Future research directions emphasize quantum dot-based self-repair systems, bio-hybrid catalysts, and AI-driven adaptive responses, paving the way for commercially viable, self-maintaining photocatalytic systems. These innovations hold immense promise for advancing sustainable energy production, environmental remediation, and carbon fixation, offering critical solutions to global sustainability challenges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy crisis and escalating environmental concerns have intensified the search for sustainable energy solutions. Among the most promising technologies for solar energy conversion and storage is photocatalysis, a process with the potential to revolutionize renewable energy applications, such as hydrogen production, solar fuel generation, and environmental remediation. Despite significant advancements in developing highly efficient photocatalytic materials, their practical implementation remains hindered by a persistent challenge: stability. Over time, many photocatalytic systems experience performance degradation due to issues such as photocorrosion, surface deactivation, and structural breakdown under prolonged exposure to light and harsh environmental conditions. Addressing these limitations is crucial for the widespread adoption of photocatalytic technologies in sustainable energy systems [1–3].

Nature offers a compelling blueprint for overcoming these challenges through self-repairing photosynthetic systems. These natural mechanisms have inspired the development of bioinspired and biomimetic photocatalysts capable of mimicking the efficiency and durability of biological light-harvesting processes. Drawing inspiration from structures like leaves, sunflowers, and algae, researchers have engineered photocatalysts with improved solar light absorption, charge transport, and catalytic efficiency [4,5]. Applications of these bioinspired materials extend beyond energy production to fields such as drug delivery, tissue engineering, and cancer therapy, although their stability and scalability remain key challenges [6]. Additionally, innovations such as molecular-modified photocathodes, which incorporate earth-abundant elements and advanced molecular interfaces, have marked a significant step toward achieving efficient artificial photosynthesis and solar-to-fuel technologies [7]. By integrating self-repair strategies and mimicking the natural resilience of photosynthetic systems, these next-generation materials aim to overcome traditional photocatalysts' limitations and advance sustainable energy solutions.

Recent breakthroughs in materials science have catalyzed the emergence of self-repairing photocatalytic systems, representing a paradigm shift in catalyst design. These materials move beyond static and passive designs, evolving into dynamic and adaptive systems capable of self-healing and recovering from degradation. By employing a diverse range of self-repair mechanisms—such as light-triggered regeneration, autonomous defect healing, and bio-inspired repair pathways—scientists have significantly enhanced the efficiency and longevity of photocatalytic materials under real-world conditions. Self-repairing photocatalysts employ a multi-scale approach to address performance challenges. At the molecular level, repair mechanisms involve the reorganization and reconstruction of active sites through catalytic cycles and specialized repair agents. At larger scales, macroscopic regeneration processes, including surface reconstruction and structural reformation, are triggered by environmental stimuli such as light or humidity. These multi-scale strategies have demonstrated remarkable success in enhancing the operational stability of photocatalytic systems, particularly in applications requiring sustained performance, such as solar fuel production and environmental remediation [1]. Structural engineering strategies have further improved photocatalytic efficiency and stability by enhancing light sensitivity, charge separation, and the integration of hybrid systems. At the molecular level, techniques such as remodeling crystal lattice structures, tailoring surface interfaces, and designing active reaction sites have broadened light absorption and mitigated electron-hole recombination. For large-scale applications, efforts focus on optimizing synthesis methods, improving support deposition techniques, and designing systems for enhanced mass transfer and photon absorption [8].

This review provides a comprehensive analysis of recent progress in self-repairing photocatalytic systems, focusing on the fundamental principles underlying self-repair mechanisms, such as molecular-level repair, surface reconstruction, and bio-inspired adaptive interfaces. Particular emphasis is placed on bio-inspired approaches, including artificial enzyme cascades and smart interfaces with autonomous regeneration, which offer transformative potential for improving photocatalytic efficiency and durability. By highlighting these innovations, this review underscores the transformative role of self-repairing photocatalysts in addressing global challenges in energy and environmental sustainability. Looking ahead, research will continue to focus on optimizing self-repair mechanisms, boosting system efficiency, and developing cost-effective solutions. The future of self-healing photocatalysts lies in their integration into scalable systems for practical use, with immense potential to address some of the most pressing environmental and energy challenges. This review aims to highlight recent progress, current challenges, and future opportunities in self-healing photocatalytic systems, particularly in relation to their real-world applications in sustainable energy and environmental protection.

Self-healing photocatalytic systems represent a transformative advancement in photocatalyst technology, inspired by biological processes that enable organisms to maintain efficiency under stress. These systems are designed to autonomously regenerate damaged components, ensuring long-term stability and performance across various environmental conditions. The fundamental mechanisms driving self-healing in photocatalytic systems can be broadly categorized into molecular-level restoration, surface reconstruction, and macroscopic regeneration processes, with bio-inspired strategies playing a crucial role in enhancing their functionality.

At the molecular level, self-healing mechanisms often involve dynamic catalytic cycles that facilitate the reorganization of active sites or the introduction of repair agents that specifically target degraded regions. These cycles mimic natural processes, such as the repair cycle of Photosystem II (PSII) in photosynthetic organisms. PSII, particularly the D1 protein, is highly susceptible to oxidative damage under intense light, and organisms have evolved mechanisms to replace this protein to maintain photosynthetic efficiency. This process involves the degradation of the damaged protein, followed by the insertion of a newly synthesized D1 protein and reassembly of the PSII complex (Fig. 1 ). Similar self-healing strategies can be employed in photocatalysts, where damaged active sites or catalytic centers are selectively restored, ensuring long-term functionality [9]. In addition to molecular-level restoration, larger-scale processes such as surface reconstruction and structural reorganization are also critical in photocatalytic systems. These processes can be triggered by external stimuli such as light or environmental conditions, enabling the recovery of the photocatalytic material's surface. This recovery enhances its ability to absorb photons, separate charges, and catalyze reactions. These macroscopic self-healing mechanisms are akin to how PSII reorganizes its thylakoid membrane to optimize the repair process. The reorganization of repair components in photocatalysts similarly aims to improve system stability by restructuring damaged areas to restore functionality [6]. (See Fig. 2.)

Bio-inspired self-healing mechanisms are increasingly integrated into photocatalytic systems, based on natural processes like the repair cycles observed in PSII and other enzymatic systems. These mechanisms include light-triggered regeneration, autonomous defect healing, and the use of chaperone proteins or adaptive interfaces that continuously monitor and restore material integrity. Just as PSII utilizes molecular chaperones to detect and respond to subtle conformational changes in the D1 protein, self-healing photocatalysts often incorporate similar repair agents that autonomously monitor the material's integrity and trigger restorative actions as needed [11,12]. A functional analysis of the molecular photosynthetic apparatus suggests that, similar to natural photosynthesis, individual components of artificial photocatalytic systems must perform distinct tasks, such as light absorption, charge separation, electron transport, and catalytic turnover. Nature demonstrates that even the most advanced systems experience degradation. For example, green plants have developed sophisticated self-healing mechanisms to address the continuous degradation of key subunits within the photosynthetic machinery, thereby enhancing its longevity (Fig. 1) [13]. This concept of molecular self-healing in natural systems offers valuable insights for artificial systems, with promising self-healing materials already being used in applications like water oxidation [14]. The next steps in improving artificial photocatalytic systems involve better understanding their degradation pathways and implementing guided self-healing strategies to restore functionality [15].

The repair cycle of natural photosynthesis is frequently cited as inspiration for artificial systems and will be briefly outlined here (Fig. 1) [16]. The D1 protein, a core component of the polypeptide structure in Photosystem II (PSII), is particularly vulnerable to high light intensities. Under these conditions, the D1 protein can be replaced as frequently as twice per hour. While the molecular processes triggering photoinhibition due to D1 protein photodegradation remain debated (Φ ≈ 10−7) [16], the mechanisms for reactivating the photosynthetic apparatus are well understood. Upon D1 protein damage (step i in Fig. 1), phosphorylation leads to the disassembly of PSII and degradation of the dysfunctional D1 protein by proteases (step ii). This is followed by the synthesis of a new D1 protein and the reassembly of functional PSII (step iii). The formation of the functional oxygen-evolving complex (OEC) using Mn(II) and Ca(II) ions (step iv) is light-triggered. The stepwise assembly of the OEC begins with PSII-mediated oxidation of Mn(II) to Mn(III), which coordinates further Mn(II) and Ca(II) ions, ultimately forming the functional CaMn₄O₅ cluster. Thus, the natural repair of the photosynthetic apparatus involves both photoindependent and photodependent processes, a strategy that is also applied in artificial systems.

In this context, self-healing specifically refers to in situ repair of the catalyst under operating conditions without requiring an external stimulus, a concept widely reviewed for water-oxidizing catalysts [14]. Systems that require external input (e.g., chemicals, light) under non-operating conditions for reactivation do not exhibit true self-healing properties and must be repaired actively (e.g., by human intervention). The term “repair” in this context refers to any chemical process that regenerates the catalyst to its initial or slightly altered form, thereby restoring catalytic activity.

Building on the foundational principles of self-healing photocatalytic systems, recent advancements have focused on overcoming challenges such as rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and improving the durability and stability of photocatalytic surfaces under real-world conditions. A promising strategy in this regard is the development of multifunctional hybrid materials that combine self-healing properties with enhanced photocatalytic performance. Another critical advancement is the integration of built-in electric fields (BIEFs) with photocatalytic materials. BIEFs, formed through polarization and the interaction of different material phases (e.g., in heterojunctions), play a pivotal role in enhancing charge separation and reducing recombination. A review by Luo et al. [17] emphasizes the synergistic effect of external fields and BIEFs, which can be further optimized through self-healing mechanisms. These self-healing processes are often driven by piezoelectric, pyroelectric, or ferroelectric effects, allowing the photocatalyst to recover its active state and maintain high efficiency even under fluctuating environmental conditions. Luo et al. highlight how BIEFs optimize photocatalytic reactions by enhancing charge transfer rates and facilitating light absorption at the photocatalyst surface. These structural and interfacial characteristics are critical for improving the overall performance of photocatalytic systems.

The integration of bio-inspired self-healing mechanisms with advanced materials has also led to the development of adaptive photocatalytic systems. These systems utilize light-triggered regeneration, autonomous defect healing, and adaptive interfaces to continuously monitor and restore material integrity. For example, inspired by the repair cycle of Photosystem II (PSII), researchers have developed photocatalytic systems that incorporate molecular chaperones or repair agents to detect and respond to subtle conformational changes, ensuring long-term functionality. By combining multifunctional materials, built-in electric fields, and bio-inspired repair mechanisms, these systems offer sustainable solutions for environmental remediation, energy production, and infrastructure durability. The next section will explore specific applications and future directions for these innovative materials.

The development of self-repairing photocatalysts involves various design strategies aimed at enhancing their longevity and efficiency. These strategies often draw inspiration from natural processes and integrate advanced material design techniques. Below, key approaches used in the design of self-repairing photocatalysts, focusing on molecular-level repair, surface reconstruction, bio-inspired adaptive interfaces, smart material architectures, and the integration of multiple repair mechanisms is investigated.

Molecular-level self-repair in photocatalysts focuses on the ability of individual molecules to detect and repair damage at the atomic or molecular scale. This capability is essential for maintaining long-term photocatalytic activity, particularly under harsh operational conditions where photogenerated radicals, UV irradiation, and reactive intermediates can degrade active sites.

This repair is typically achieved by incorporating self-healing motifs—molecular structures engineered to respond to damage through reversible chemical reactions, such as bond cleavage and reformation. For example, dynamic covalent bonds (e.g., disulfide, imine, and boronic ester linkages) and supramolecular interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, π–π stacking, and metal–ligand coordination) have been exploited in the design of repairable photocatalytic systems.

One prominent class of materials that demonstrates such self-repair capacity is metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). These materials offer a modular platform where both the metal centers and organic linkers can be tailored for stimuli-responsive behavior. In some MOFs, damage to active catalytic sites induces a local shift in coordination geometry, triggering a self-corrective rearrangement or ligand exchange that restores functionality. For instance, light-induced ligand displacement followed by spontaneous rebinding has been reported in zirconium-based MOFs used for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. In such systems, the light-activated catalytic cycle itself drives the repair, closely resembling the auto-regulatory nature of Photosystem II.

In polymeric photocatalysts, especially those based on conjugated polymers or covalent organic frameworks (COFs), damage can initiate chain reorganization or intramolecular energy redistribution that stabilizes the material. These systems have shown promise for photocatalytic CO₂ reduction and dye degradation, where recovery of the photoactive π-conjugated network is key. For example, specific polymeric photocatalysts with built-in Diels–Alder adducts can undergo reversible cycloaddition reactions upon heating, repairing the disrupted backbone and restoring charge transport pathways.

Moreover, redox-active sites, such as transition metal complexes embedded in a polymeric matrix, can undergo valence-state oscillations that facilitate not only photocatalytic turnover but also self-healing. When the catalytic site becomes over-oxidized or poisoned, surrounding redox-active ligands or co-factors can donate electrons or rearrange coordination geometry, effectively resetting the site to its active state.

Nature's approach—particularly the PSII D1 protein turnover—offers a conceptual template. Analogous artificial systems may use sacrificial ligands or photoswitchable components that detect damage and enable reconstitution of the catalytic architecture. For example, peptide-inspired linkers in artificial systems can dissociate upon oxidative stress and reassemble through template-directed synthesis or through external triggers such as light or pH changes.

Importantly, computational modeling and in situ spectroscopic techniques (e.g., operando XPS, EPR, or ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy) have begun to elucidate the fundamental steps of such self-repair events. This mechanistic understanding enables the rational design of next-generation materials with predictive self-healing behavior, bridging the gap between biological mimicry and scalable artificial systems.

In summary, molecular-level self-repair mechanisms offer a powerful route to extend the operational lifespan of photocatalysts. Future innovations will likely focus on synergistic designs combining dynamic covalent chemistry, coordinative adaptivity, and phototriggered self-repair to create robust systems capable of long-term, autonomous operation in complex environments [18,19].

Surface reconstruction and regeneration are essential for maintaining the photocatalytic performance of materials subjected to long-term operation in chemically aggressive or photochemically active environments. Over time, the active sites on a photocatalyst's surface can become deactivated due to fouling, oxidation, surface restructuring, or leaching of essential components. These degradation pathways directly impact charge carrier separation, light absorption, and the surface redox reactions necessary for photocatalysis. To address this challenge, photocatalysts can be designed with dynamic surface properties that allow for structural reorganization or chemical renewal at the interface with the surrounding medium. This can be accomplished via two main strategies: (1) self-regenerating surface chemistry, and (2) externally triggered surface reconstruction. In the first approach, materials are engineered to self-heal through redox cycles or ion-exchange mechanisms. For example, certain perovskite oxides and layered double hydroxides (LDHs) can spontaneously exchange surface ions (e.g., O2−, H+, or metal cations) with the surrounding medium to regenerate lost activity. This exchange not only restores surface functionality but also helps remove adsorbed reaction byproducts that may otherwise block active sites. In photocatalytic water splitting, SrTiO₃ and LaFeO₃ have demonstrated the ability to re-form surface hydroxyl groups and oxygen vacancies essential for water oxidation, even after partial deactivation.

In TiO₂-based systems, one of the most extensively studied photocatalysts, surface regeneration can be enhanced through the incorporation of oxygen-rich coatings or defect engineering. These strategies promote the creation of oxygen vacancies or anatase-rutile interfacial junctions, which act as self-adjusting sites to trap and release electrons and holes efficiently. Upon exposure to light and reactive species, these surface sites can dynamically reconfigure, preventing deep deactivation. Moreover, post-synthetic treatments such as UV-assisted annealing or hydrothermal activation have been used to rejuvenate spent TiO₂ photocatalysts by removing surface-bound contaminants and restoring crystallinity.

Another promising route involves the application of self-healing surface coatings, such as photoactive polymer layers or MOF-derived thin films, which serve dual roles: protecting the underlying photocatalyst from corrosion and enabling autonomous surface renewal. These coatings can undergo reversible chemical transformations, such as bond exchange or ligand migration, which help repair defects caused by photodegradation or mechanical stress. Additionally, noble-metal-modified surfaces (e.g., Pt/TiO₂, Au/g-C₃N₄) have shown surface regeneration behavior through plasmonic heating and localized redox cycling. When illuminated, localized heating induces surface diffusion and reorganization, which can re-expose buried catalytic sites or drive sintered nanoparticles to redisperse into active forms.

Importantly, advanced in situ characterization techniques such as scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM), ambient pressure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AP-XPS), and operando AFM provide real-time insight into surface changes, helping to design systems with tunable reconstruction behavior. These findings have enabled the development of adaptive surfaces, which can modulate their structure and chemistry depending on environmental conditions such as pH, light intensity, and the presence of target pollutants. Therefore, surface reconstruction and regeneration offer critical pathways to extend the longevity and performance of photocatalysts. By harnessing material adaptability and surface chemistry dynamics, researchers can create systems that not only resist deactivation but actively restore themselves during operation—paving the way for self-sustaining photocatalytic technologies in water treatment, energy conversion, and environmental remediation. [20,21].

Inspired by biological systems, bio-inspired adaptive interfaces are being integrated into photocatalytic systems to enable real-time, dynamic responses to environmental stressors such as UV radiation, chemical fouling, or structural degradation. These interfaces mimic natural processes like wound healing in plants, tissue regeneration in animals, and protein repair in chloroplasts, by incorporating structural and chemical cues that guide self-repair behavior. In practice, these adaptive interfaces are often constructed from stimuli-responsive hydrogels, self-healing polymers, or bio-derived composites that respond to changes in light intensity, temperature, pH, or redox conditions. For instance, hydrogels functionalized with redox-active groups can swell or shrink in response to electron flux, closing micro-cracks or reestablishing lost conductivity in the photocatalytic matrix. Similarly, dynamic covalent bonds in self-healing polymers can reversibly dissociate and reform, enabling repair of both physical damage and chemical defects.

A notable example is the integration of light-sensitive motifs, such as azobenzene or spiropyran groups, into photocatalytic polymers. These groups undergo photoisomerization under specific wavelengths, which can trigger conformational changes in the polymer backbone—effectively reorganizing the catalytic interface for restored functionality. Moreover, bio-inspired chaperone-like molecules embedded in the catalyst surface can identify defective catalytic sites and initiate local structural adjustments or even redox reactions to regenerate activity, mimicking the D1 protein turnover in Photosystem II (PSII).

Recent research has shown that embedding PSII-mimetic peptides into photocatalyst matrices can further improve long-term operational stability by maintaining the spatial arrangement of catalytic centers and promoting self-repair through selective binding and reassembly. These adaptive systems not only restore catalytic performance but can also prevent further degradation by adapting their surface chemistry in real-time.

Ultimately, the development of bio-inspired adaptive interfaces represents a significant leap toward autonomous photocatalytic systems capable of continuous operation in fluctuating and harsh environments. Their multifunctionality—combining sensing, repair initiation, and structural adaptation—marks a convergence between biomimetic design and advanced materials science for the next generation of robust photocatalysts [22,23].

Smart material architectures represent a forward-thinking strategy in photocatalyst design, integrating multiple functionalities that enable autonomous self-repair, adaptability, and environmental responsiveness. These architectures are engineered using a combination of stimuli-responsive layers, multi-phase composites, and hierarchical nano-to-microstructural organization, which collectively contribute to enhanced durability and sustained performance under operational stress.

A defining feature of these systems is their ability to sense, respond, and adapt to external stimuli such as light intensity, pH, temperature, or mechanical strain. For example, thermo-responsive polymer layers embedded in photocatalytic films can expand or contract in response to localized heating, thereby facilitating crack sealing or pore regeneration. Similarly, pH-responsive inorganic frameworks can undergo reversible structural transitions, allowing dynamic reorganization of catalytic active sites and regeneration of degraded regions.

Hierarchical structuring, achieved through techniques such as 3D printing, electrospinning, or layer-by-layer assembly, plays a pivotal role in directing the flow of reactants and minimizing diffusion limitations while enabling spatially selective self-healing. These structures often mimic biological systems, where layered defense and repair mechanisms (e.g., skin, bone) respond to localized damage. For instance, 3D-printed TiO₂ composites with biomimetic channels have been shown to promote both photocatalytic performance and self-repair efficiency, due to their ability to distribute mechanical stress and facilitate the migration of healing agents to damaged zones.

Advanced architectures may also incorporate sacrificial repair phases or reconfigurable bonds (such as disulfide linkages, hydrogen bonding networks, or reversible metal-ligand coordination) within their frameworks. Upon exposure to triggers such as UV light or oxidizing environments, these features enable the selective removal of damaged components and reassembly of the photocatalytic interface, effectively restoring functionality without external intervention.

Furthermore, multi-functional layers embedded within smart architectures can serve not only as photocatalysts but also as protective coatings, damage sensors, and repair initiators. For example, layered structures combining photocatalytic oxides with conductive polymers allow for self-monitoring via electrical signal changes and can activate in situ repair processes when degradation is detected.

Overall, smart material architectures offer a powerful platform for developing resilient, self-sustaining photocatalysts tailored for long-term deployment in real-world environmental remediation. By combining precision fabrication methods with nature-inspired repair strategies, these materials pave the way toward fully autonomous, high-efficiency catalytic systems [24,25].

The integration of multiple self-repair mechanisms into a single photocatalytic system represents a cutting-edge approach to overcoming performance degradation under complex and variable operational conditions. Rather than relying on a singular strategy, these hybrid systems combine complementary repair processes—such as molecular-level repair, surface reconstruction, and adaptive interfaces—to enhance both the resilience and functionality of photocatalysts over prolonged usage.

This multi-pronged approach allows for targeted and stage-specific recovery, whereby distinct types of damage—such as photochemical degradation, mechanical stress, or surface fouling—can be addressed by different repair modules. For instance, a photocatalyst may incorporate: Molecular-level repair motifs, such as dynamic covalent bonds or metal-ligand exchange centers, that detect and re-form broken linkages in the photocatalytic framework; Self-regenerating surfaces, enabled by ion exchange or redox-driven reconstruction, to restore active sites deactivated by adsorbed intermediates or contaminants; Bio-inspired adaptive matrices, such as hydrogels or responsive polymers, which adjust material morphology or porosity in response to environmental cues (e.g., temperature, pH, or chemical load).

Such synergistic integration not only extends the service life of the material but also maintains high catalytic activity under fluctuating environmental conditions. A notable example includes hybrid photocatalysts composed of TiO₂ nanoparticles embedded in a self-healing polymer matrix that responds to UV-triggered damage through a dual process: first, the reversible rearrangement of polymeric chains repairs mechanical cracks, followed by surface site regeneration via Ti4+/Ti3+ redox cycling to recover photocatalytic activity. In more advanced systems, multi-layered architectures have been developed, in which each layer serves a distinct role—such as outer protective/self-healing coatings, inner active layers for photoreactions, and embedded sensing elements that trigger localized repair responses. These designs emulate biological systems that deploy tiered repair hierarchies, akin to cellular regeneration processes or the photosynthetic apparatus of plants. Recent studies have demonstrated that such integrated systems outperform traditional single-mechanism photocatalysts in both photocatalytic degradation rates and operational stability, especially when subjected to stressors such as UV radiation, high salinity, or mechanical abrasion. These findings underscore the promise of multi-repair strategies in developing next-generation photocatalysts for sustainable environmental remediation [26,27].

Self-healing photocatalysts hold transformative potential in various practical applications, particularly in renewable energy production and environmental remediation. Their ability to autonomously regenerate enhances the operational stability of photocatalytic systems under prolonged exposure to harsh conditions. In solar fuel generation, self-healing photocatalysts offer a promising solution to issues of catalyst degradation, enabling more efficient and sustainable hydrogen production through water splitting. In environmental remediation, these photocatalysts contribute to the degradation of persistent pollutants, ensuring long-term efficacy in wastewater treatment and CO2 reduction. In this section, we discuss the application of self-healing photocatalysts in these areas, highlighting how their regenerative capabilities improve both performance and longevity. By mimicking natural self-repair mechanisms, these advanced materials not only enhance the lifespan of photocatalytic systems but also reduce maintenance costs, making them viable for large-scale applications and commercial use.

Self-repairing photocatalysts represent a groundbreaking advancement in solar fuel production, offering the potential to address challenges such as catalyst degradation and efficiency loss over time. By mimicking natural photosynthesis, these systems can autonomously repair damage caused by prolonged light exposure, ensuring sustained performance and scalability for large-scale energy applications. This section explores two key studies that demonstrate the potential of self-repairing photocatalysts for hydrogen generation and enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The integration of molecularly tailored catalysts with 2D materials holds immense potential for advancing photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reactions (HER). Due to their ultrathin dimensions, 2D materials are uniquely suited to host small-scale catalysts, including single atoms [28] and molecular complexes. These catalysts exhibit superior interaction and infiltration within the layered structures of 2D materials. Notably, hydrogenase and nitrogenase enzymes, with Fe, Ni, and Mo active centers, have inspired the design of molecular MoSx-based complexes for HER due to their catalytic efficiency and environmental compatibility [29,30].

Amorphous MoSx films, have been studied as HER catalysts, often feature thicknesses of 40–50 nm, which can hinder light absorption and photocatalytic efficiency. In contrast, molecularly thin films provide optimal light penetration, improving the overall performance of photocatalysts. Recently, Mo-based complexes such as Mo₃S₄, (Mo₂S₁₂)2−, and (Mo₃S₁₃)2− have emerged as promising HER catalysts due to their ability to mimic MoS₂ edge sites [31–34]. These complexes offer a sustainable alternative to noble metals, demonstrating both high catalytic efficiency and stability in acidic environments. Despite their promise, the role of oxo-thio complexes, which form through hydrolysis in alkaline media, remains underexplored. Initial studies suggest that these complexes could enhance HER efficiency by leveraging redox reactions involving MoO groups and S₂2− anions [35–37].

Scaling up the use of Mo thio complexes for hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) requires overcoming several challenges, including catalyst durability and stability, defect passivation, and selective catalyst anchoring. Long-term operation demands catalysts that resist desorption and degradation, which can be achieved through chemical bonding between catalysts and substrates, ensuring enhanced durability [38]. Surface defects on 2D materials like WSe₂ can accelerate charge carrier recombination, reducing photocatalytic performance, but passivating these defects significantly enhances the efficiency of photoelectrodes. Effective anchoring strategies must ensure that active sites remain accessible while stabilizing the catalyst on the 2D substrate, with ligand interactions with WSe₂ defects or cations providing robust anchoring and healing defects, thereby enhancing the material's optoelectronic properties [39]. To address these challenges, in a work [40] Mo thio complexes were synthesized and deposited onto WSe₂ nanosheets using a sustainable, solvent-free dip-coating method, enabling the selective adsorption of catalytically active thio and oxo-thio species. Mass spectroscopy (ESI-MS) and XPS analyses revealed a distribution of thio and oxo-thio species in the films, with higher S/Mo ratios indicating an enriched presence of catalytically active complexes. Films prepared via dip-coating exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic performance compared to those formed by drop-casting, as dip-coating allowed for the progressive selection of active complexes, resulting in enhanced HER efficiency. Photocurrent densities reached up to 2.5 mA cm−2 under 1-sun illumination—substantially higher than those achieved with noble metal catalysts like PtCu (0.4 mA cm−2) or MoS₂ nanocrystals [41]—attributed to the reduced density of charge recombination centers and effective defect passivation.

Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations provided further insights into the interaction between Mo complexes and WSe₂ substrates. The Mo complexes weakly bind with defect-free pristine WSe₂ but strongly adsorb onto W-vacancy sites, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . Interestingly, (MoS₄)2−, (MoS₃O)2− monomers, or small-sized dimers like (Mo₂S₆O₂)2− bind more strongly to point defects than larger complexes such as (Mo₂S₁₂)2−. Oxo-thio complexes, in particular, exhibit stronger adsorption compared to their thio analogues on WSe₂ substrates containing Se or W vacancies. The interaction between W-vacancies and Mo complexes, such as (MoOS₃)2−, (Mo₂S₆O₂)2−, and (Mo₂S₁₂)2−, results in the formation of new SeS bonds (d_Se-S = 2.3 Å), as shown in Fig. 3a. This strong adsorption of Mo complexes onto WSe₂ nanosheets, particularly at Se and W vacancies, passivates surface defects and enhances photocatalytic performance. The stronger adsorption displayed by Mo monomers or small dimers like (Mo₂S₆O₂)2−, compared to pure (Mo₂S₁₂)2−, is consistent with experimental results, highlighting their crucial role in defect passivation.

In terms of catalytic activity, DFT calculations reveal that dimers such as (Mo₂S₆O₂)2− and (Mo₂S₁₂)2− exhibit optimal performance in a free-standing or low-adsorption regime on defect-free WSe₂ substrates (Fig. 3c). In contrast, strongly adsorbed complexes like (MoS₄)2−, (Mo₂S₆O₂)2−, and (Mo₂S₁₂)2− on W-vacancy-containing WSe₂ monolayers show ΔG_ads(H) values far from the optimum of the Volcano curve [39], indicating reduced catalytic efficiency. These calculations suggest that water-soluble Mo-oxo-thio complexes consist of three classes of molecular species: (i) Mo monomers with low catalytic activity in their free-standing configuration but high adsorption energy onto WSe₂; (ii) (Mo₂S₁₂)2− dimers with high catalytic activity for HER but low affinity for WSe₂; and (iii) (MoS₃O)2− oxo-thio monomers with strong adsorption toward WSe₂ and reasonable catalytic activity in their anchored configuration. These Mo oxo-thio complexes, capable of both passivating surface defects and displaying catalytic activity, can be described as healing catalysts.

The study demonstrates significant improvements in photoelectrochemical performance through the incorporation of thio-Mo complexes as co-catalysts on WSe₂ photoelectrodes, with key findings including the suppression of charge recombination, evidenced by a substantial reduction in current spikes and enhanced charge transfer efficiency (ηₜᵣ = iₛₛ/iₜ₌₀) [42]. A distinct change in the slope of the i(V) curves under illumination compared to dark conditions for the co-catalyst-coated WSe₂ photoelectrode underscores the reduction in recombination rates and the activation of efficient charge transfer mechanisms. The emergence of a clear photoluminescence (PL) peak at 1.43 eV in Mo thio-complex-coated samples, compared to the absence of PL in the bare electrode, further supports defect passivation. Real-time hydrogen evolution measurements via gas chromatography confirmed a high Faradaic yield of approximately 97 % at a current density of j = 0.25 mA cm−2 (E_bias = −0.1 V vs. NHE) [41], while IPCE measurements revealed a maximum efficiency of 9 % at λ = 760 nm (1.63 eV), corresponding to the excitonic peak of WSe₂. The co-catalyst-coated electrode achieved a broad IPCE response across the wavelength range of 810–490 nm (1.53–2.53 eV), while the bare WSe₂ photoelectrode exhibited negligible IPCE values (∼0.2 %) [37]. The integration of thio-Mo complexes on WSe₂ photoelectrodes not only enhances charge transfer and suppresses recombination but also significantly boosts photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency, underscoring the pivotal role of surface modification and co-catalyst engineering in advancing photoelectrode technologies for sustainable energy applications. The distinct and complementary roles of thio- and oxo-thio-Mo complexes as healing additives and catalysts highlight their potential for scalable application in hydrogen production.

Building on the remarkable efficiency of Mo thio and oxo-thio complexes in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, the integration of built-in electric fields (BIEFs) and self-healing mechanisms offers a transformative approach to further enhance charge separation, light absorption, and catalytic stability, paving the way for scalable and sustainable fuel production systems. The establishment of built-in electric fields (BIEFs) significantly enhances the efficiency of separating photogenerated carriers, thereby improving overall photocatalytic performance [17]. BIEFs optimize light absorption and redox potential, making them crucial for applications in sustainable fuel production, environmental remediation, and carbon fixation. This review classifies BIEFs into polarization BIEFs (arising from natural or artificially induced charge imbalances) and interface BIEFs (common in heterojunctions like p-n, type-II, Z-scheme, S-scheme, and van der Waals junctions). A key advancement is the integration of self-healing BIEFs, which address the limitations of static BIEFs (e.g., electrostatic shielding) by leveraging piezoelectric, pyroelectric, and ferroelectric effects. These self-healing mechanisms, induced by periodic external forces (stress, thermal, or electric fields), enable dynamic charge-discharge cycles, enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. Such innovations are pivotal for developing robust photocatalytic systems that mimic natural photosynthesis, offering sustainable solutions for energy production and environmental challenges [17].

Photocatalytic systems for environmental remediation leverage self-repairing mechanisms to degrade pollutants, including organic contaminants and heavy metals in water and air. These systems maintain their photocatalytic activity over time, making them ideal for treating large volumes of contaminated water or air without significant loss of efficiency. The ability to self-repair after exposure to harmful pollutants extends their lifespan and effectiveness, addressing critical challenges in environmental cleanup. Additionally, bio-inspired approaches, such as mimicking natural processes for pollutant degradation, further enhance the sustainability and efficiency of these systems. This section explores the integration of self-healing photocatalysts and bio-inspired designs in environmental remediation, highlighting their potential for scalable and sustainable applications.

While TiO₂ remains one of the most widely used photocatalysts, its effectiveness is primarily limited to the UV region of the light spectrum, which restricts its applications under natural sunlight [43–45]. To address this limitation, significant efforts have focused on expanding TiO₂’s photoabsorption capabilities into the visible light region and developing novel non-TiO₂ photocatalysts [46–48]. Among these, cobalt monoxide (CoO), a p-type semiconductor with a band gap of 2.2–2.8 eV, has emerged as a promising material due to its high photocatalytic activity, chemical stability, and potential applications in environmental disinfection [49–51]. Another material of interest is metallic silver (Ag) nanoparticles, known for their antimicrobial properties and ability to enhance visible light absorption through the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect [52–54]. The binding of Ag nanoparticles to metal oxide substrates also mitigates the environmental risks associated with the release of Ag ions [55,56], making Ag-modified CoO photocatalysts a significant advancement for synergistic antibacterial activity.

In a work [57], nitrogen-doped CoO (CoO-N) photocatalysts were synthesized using a supercritical solvothermal route in ethanol fluid, a method that ensures uniform crystal nucleation and stable interactions between components by eliminating surface tension. Nitrogen doping enhances the specific surface area of CoO-N and improves its light absorption in the visible region. Further modification with Ag nanoparticles, deposited via photo-reduction under visible-light irradiation, enhances light absorption and promotes efficient separation of photo-induced charge carriers. The resulting Ag/CoO-N composites exhibit superior photocatalytic performance, particularly in the inactivation of Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii), a pathogen commonly found in medical wastewater that poses significant health risks.

The photocatalytic sterilization of A. baumannii under visible-light irradiation is driven by the high light-trapping capability and efficient charge separation of the Ag/CoO-N composites. Photo-induced electrons act as the primary active species, leading to the mineralization of the bacterial cell membrane, potassium ion (K+) leakage, and structural disruption. This process impairs the bacterial genetic material (DNA and RNA) and weakens its self-repair mechanism, ultimately resulting in cell death. The 3.0 % Ag/CoO-N-5 composite demonstrated the highest photocatalytic inactivation efficiency, outperforming both CoO and CoO-N samples.

The synergistic effects of Ag nanoparticles and CoO-N are key to the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The Ag nanoparticles improve light absorption and charge separation, while the nitrogen doping increases the specific surface area and visible-light response. This work highlights the potential of Ag/CoO-N composites for environmental microorganism control, offering valuable insights for advanced antimicrobial and environmental remediation technologies. By combining the unique properties of Ag and CoO-N, these composites represent a significant step forward in developing efficient and sustainable photocatalytic systems for environmental applications.

Microbial infections pose significant threats to human health, global economies, and mental well-being [58]. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the severity of secondary infections and antimicrobial resistance (AMR) [59,60]. Bacterial infections in chronic wounds, especially in diabetic patients, can accelerate biofilm formation and impede wound healing [61]. Traditional treatments, such as antibiotics and hydrogels, have limitations, including poor wound healing improvement, cytotoxicity, and instability [62–67]. A combination of anti-microbial and anti-inflammatory effects is essential for advancing wound healing therapies. Metal-based nanomaterials have been explored for bacterial eradication by inducing oxidative stress, but challenges such as toxicity and stability persist [68–70]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly carbon dots (CDs), have gained attention for their selective electron transfer and photocatalytic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [71–75]. Doping CDs with heteroatoms enhances their enzyme-mimicking capabilities, and their ability to absorb broad-spectrum light facilitates ROS production [76–79].

Ina study, CDsomes—amphipathic carbon dot liposomes—were developed through the pyrolysis of triolein [80]. These CDsomes exhibit light-triggered properties, switching between “on” and “off” states upon ultraviolet (UV) and green light irradiation. In the “on” state, CDsomes catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) formation via UV light, while in the “off” state, they generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH) through peroxidase-mimicking activity under green light. This light-driven cascade reaction enhances antimicrobial activity by generating cytotoxic ROS. The CDsomes demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, minimal hemolytic activity, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, including against multi-drug resistant bacteria. In vivo experiments showed that CDsomes, activated by sequential light treatments, effectively eradicated microbial pathogens in methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA)-infected wounds in diabetic mice. The CDsomes also exhibited anti-inflammatory and angiogenic properties, accelerating wound healing by promoting epithelial reconstruction and collagen formation. The programmable, light-guided CDsomes hold great promise as a novel therapeutic strategy for treating infected chronic wounds.

Amphipathic carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through controlled pyrolysis of triolein and hydrolysis, forming self-assembled liposome-like structures known as CDsomes (Fig. 4A). The CDs' surfaces, decorated with asymmetric hydrophobic chains, facilitated their hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity-driven assembly into nanostructures. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) analysis confirmed the presence of functional groups (CO, CH, C-OH), while Raman and X-ray diffraction (XRD) data indicated the graphitic nature of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) revealed a high proportion of C=C/C–C bonds (62.1 %). CD somes exhibited a negative zeta potential (−31.4 ± 3.2 mV) and hydrodynamic diameters of ∼98.8 nm in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed CDsomes as liposome-like structures (80–100 nm) in PBS (Fig. 1B). UV–Vis spectroscopy displayed absorption peaks at 240 nm (π → π* transitions) and 290 nm (n → π* transitions). CDsomes showed excitation-dependent fluorescence, with maximum emission at 482 nm upon 400 nm excitation, and a quantum yield of 4.12 %. The fluorescence depended on varying polycyclic aromatic clusters, edge defects, and emissive traps, with high photostability confirmed by unaltered morphology and size after UV and green light irradiation.

CD somes exhibited reversible fluorescence states under sequential light irradiation. UV light (360–390 nm) induced a blue fluorescent “on” state, while green light (525–545 nm) shifted them to a red, non-fluorescent “off” state. This reversible switching was attributed to the electron transfer between high-energy traps within the self-assembled CDs, facilitating mutual electron transfer. The CDsomes' structural stability and light-responsive behavior make them promising candidates for photocatalytic applications and controlled therapeutic use. By combining their photocatalytic and peroxidase-like activities, CDsomes offer a versatile platform for addressing microbial infections and promoting wound healing, demonstrating their potential as a groundbreaking therapeutic strategy.

In recent years, the development of self-cleaning surfaces has garnered significant scientific and public interest, particularly due to their potential applications in environmental and industrial sectors. Light-induced photoreactive coatings represent one of the primary classes of such surfaces, capable of chemically degrading organic materials under illumination [81]. To enhance the performance of these coatings, modifications to the catalyst composition allow for an extended absorption spectrum of the semiconductor, improving their efficiency across a broader range of light wavelengths [82]. Another promising category is water-repellent coatings, with dual-functionality surfaces that exhibit both superhydrophobic and photocatalytic behavior. Recent advancements in these materials include composite layers where a low-energy polymer acts as a binder for photocatalyst particles. The photocatalysts are responsible for the photoactivity, while the polymer matrix ensures adequate surface roughness [83]. However, the durability of these nanoscopic, rough surfaces remain a concern, as they are prone to degradation over time, diminishing their long-term effectiveness [84].

To address these challenges, the nature-inspired self-healing ability of artificial coatings has been proposed as a solution. In a work the immobilization of ZnO particles within a low-energy fluoropolymer binder to produce bifunctional thin layers, achieving both photoactivity and superhydrophobicity was demonstrated [85]. In continue [86], the photoactivity into the visible range by incorporating plasmonic Ag-TiO₂ photocatalysts was extend, while maintaining the superhydrophobic characteristics. Additionally, a mechanism by which air humidity catalyzed the hydrolysis/self-condensation reaction of hydrophobic DDSiCl₃ silane molecules was proposed, resulting in self-assembled micro-protrusions on the surface. This process contributes to the creation of self-healing, superhydrophobic surfaces. This study prepares a multifunctional coating material with photoreactive, superhydrophobic, and intelligent self-healing properties.

The wetting properties of the thin films were further investigated, with surface roughness playing a critical role. As shown in Fig. 5a, the measured contact angles were plotted as a function of the layer composition. The initial pPFDAc layer exhibited a relatively high (=105.0°), indicative of its hydrophobic, Teflon-like nature. As the photocatalyst content increased (from left to right), the θ values also increased, indicating a decrease in the wettability of the polymer layer. Interestingly, the pure Ag-TiO₂ photocatalyst film without the polymer matrix displayed superhydrophilic characteristics (θ ≈ 0°). The optimal hydrophobicity was achieved at photocatalyst concentrations ranging from 40 to 80 wt%, with Θ values approaching 150.0°, characteristic of superhydrophobic surfaces. The total apparent surface free energies (γₛᵗ°ᵗ) of the layers were also calculated from the measured advancing (θ ₐdᵥ) and receding (θ ᵣₑc) contact angles, obtained through the Wilhelmy plate method. Fig. 5b presents the γₛᵗ°ᵗ values as a function of Ag-TiO₂ content, derived from these contact angle measurements. The calculated γₛᵗ°ᵗ values align with the static Θ data, with the minimum surface free energies (6.5–13.1 mJm−2) observed for films containing 20–80 wt% photocatalyst content. This reduction in surface energy is attributed to the roughened fluoropolymer surface, enhanced by the incorporation of Ag-TiO₂ particles (Rq = 5.2 ± 1.5 to 15.2 ± 1.2 μm). In contrast, the flat fluoropolymer layer without the Ag-TiO₂ particles had a γₛᵗ°ᵗ value of 27.3 mJm−2, typical of low-energy, Teflon-like coatings. The pure photocatalyst layer exhibited a much higher γₛᵗ°ᵗ value of 71.9 mJm−2, consistent with the superhydrophobic nature of semiconductor surfaces. These findings confirm that the wetting properties of the low-energy fluoropolymer-immobilized Ag-TiO₂ films can be tailored to exhibit a range of behaviors, from super hydrophilic to superhydrophobic, by adjusting the photocatalyst content.

Superhydrophobic materials, while promising for various applications, often suffer from surface damage due to their rough structure, which reduces their long-term effectiveness. To overcome this limitation, hybrid surfaces with self-healing properties were developed, inspired by nature. The strategy involved the hydrolysis/self-condensation reaction of DDSiCl₃ silanes, catalyzed by air humidity, to form a self-assembled siloxane layer. This process led to the formation of micro-roughened surfaces, enhancing the hydrophobicity (Θ = 124.7°), but it was insufficient to achieve superhydrophobicity. To further improve surface roughness and achieve superhydrophobicity, Ag-TiO₂ particles were incorporated into the oleogel layer. The addition of these photocatalytic particles increased the surface roughness (Rq = 15.2 ± 1.2 μm), resulting in a superhydrophobic surface with Θ = 151.6 ± 1.9°. These Ag-TiO₂ composites also exhibited photocatalytic properties, enhanced by the plasmonic effect of Ag nanoparticles. This effect shifted the absorption of the composite to the visible-light region, improving its photocatalytic activity, as indicated by the photocatalytic tests, including the ethanol degradation test and dye degradation at the solid-liquid interface.

The surface roughness of the composite films was tailored between superhydrophilic (Θ = 0°) and superhydrophobic (Θ = 150.9°) by varying the Ag-TiO₂ content. At the solid-liquid interface, the films were able to photodegrade apolar SUDAN IV dye efficiently, but degradation of the aqueous Methylene Blue dye was hindered due to insufficient surface adsorption. The films' photocatalytic activity was enhanced with increased Ag-TiO₂ content, as evidenced by higher rate constants (k') for ethanol degradation (from k' = 0.0034 min−1 to k' = 0.0495 min−1). A key aspect of these hybrid films was their self-healing ability. The moisture-induced hydrolysis and condensation of DDSiCl₃ silanes resulted in the formation of micro-roughened, hydrophobic surfaces [87]. These films exhibited quick recovery of superhydrophobic properties under ambient conditions, contributing to their long-term durability. The missing nanoroughness necessary for superhydrophobicity was achieved by incorporating Ag-TiO₂ nanoparticles into the oleogel layer, ensuring both high surface roughness and photocatalytic activity.

These hybrid surfaces present a promising approach for environmental remediation applications. The combination of superhydrophobicity, photocatalytic activity, and self-healing properties makes them ideal candidates for durable, efficient, and sustainable materials in pollution control technologies.

Self-healing hydrogels are three-dimensional cross-linked polymeric networks with high water content and the ability to autonomously repair damaged parts, inspired by the self-repairing systems found in living organisms. These hydrogels can perform autonomous self-repair based on either dynamic covalent bond (e.g., Diels-Alder reaction, coordination bond, Schiff base, boronate ester bond, and disulfide bond) or noncovalent interactions (e.g., ionic interactions, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and host-guest interactions) [88–91]. Due to their unique properties, self-healing hydrogels have found wide applications, particularly in biomedical fields, such as diabetic wound healing, cartilage tissue engineering, drug delivery, and biosensors [92–94]. Environmental contamination, particularly from chemical pollutants, remains a significant global issue and has gained considerable attention. The majority of chemical pollutants are nitroaromatic organic compounds, which are acutely toxic, have low solubility in water, and are highly stable. For example, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is widely used as a precursor for dyes, explosives, drugs, and more [95]. Exposure to 4-NP can lead to serious health issues such as skin irritation and organ dysfunction. Moreover, marine creatures exposed to this toxic chemical pose a potential risk to humans via the food chain. As a result, various techniques have been developed to eliminate these toxic chemicals from water bodies [95]. Among these techniques, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, adsorption, and the Fenton reaction are the most commonly used [96–99]. However, the adsorption method is less favorable as it generates secondary waste, which would eventually be discarded in landfills. Electrocatalysis is also limited due to its low efficiency and poor durability, making it less viable for large-scale applications. Therefore, photocatalysis is often preferred, as it requires only sunlight to drive the catalytic reactions on the catalysts [100–102].

Numerous studies have been conducted on photocatalysis for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol [103–105]. However, many of these studies focus solely on photocatalytic performance without addressing issues such as recyclability and reusability [106]. For example, Shuwanto et al. reported the fabrication of nanosized NiMg codoped Zn(O,S) photocatalysts for environmental remediation and hydrogen production under solar light irradiation [107]. Although the authors reported superior photocatalytic performance, the challenge of collecting the spent photocatalysts in real-life applications remains a critical issue. With this regard, in the other work [108] a photocatalyst embedded in a hydrogel with self-healing ability to extend its lifetime for environmental remediation was developed. In this study, a simple and facile preparation of poly-acrylic acid hydrogel embedded with TiO₂ photocatalyst (PT hydrogel) synthesized by UV light irradiation without the need for any cross-linking agents. The as-prepared hybrid catalyst was applied to reduce 4-NP to 4-AP.

The swelling degree experiment demonstrates the capability of hydrogels to absorb water into their cross-linking networks, which in this study was determined by a gravimetric method. Meanwhile, the gel fraction represents the cross-linking degree of the hydrogels. The swelling degree of PT hydrogels decreased gradually with increasing water content, as PT hydrogels could absorb water in their cross-linked networks mainly due to the hydrophilic carboxylic organic functional groups (-COO) [109]. On the other hand, the cross-linking degree of PT hydrogels increased with the increasing amount of water during synthesis. This phenomenon explains that PT hydrogels with a higher gel fraction contain denser cross-linking networks and limit water uptake, therefore resulting in lower swelling ability. Furthermore, the swelling characteristic of PT-4 hydrogel was also investigated at varied pH, solvent, AA concentration, and temperature. For swelling at different pH, the SD of PT-4 hydrogel increased from 63 % at pH 1 to the maximum of 179 % at pH 7, subsequently decreasing gradually to 103 % at pH 13. The swelling experiment in different solvents was performed in both polar and nonpolar solvents (e.g., acetone, methanol, isopropanol, ethanol, and DMSO). Among the alcohols, the SD in methanol indicates the lowest SD value, as methanol is more polar than ethanol and isopropanol, which could generate hydrogen bonding between the solvent and hydrogel, thus limiting the expansion of the hydrogel networks. On the other hand, the highest SD value is achieved by swelling in acetone due to its nature as a polar aprotic solvent that cannot form hydrogen bonds. Additionally, the SD value of PT-4 hydrogel was found to be relatively low (∼120 %) due to the disintegration of the PT-4 sample. The SD decreases gradually at higher AA concentrations, with a plateau reached at an AA concentration of 2 mL. Moreover, the SD value of PT-4 hydrogel increased with increasing swelling temperature [110].

The mechanical properties were determined by the tensile test method, which was performed on pristine and after-healed PT hydrogels. The tensile strain of pristine PT-1 hydrogel showed relatively brittle and fractured properties, as its elongation was only 31 % at a stress of 3.2 MPa. Moreover, the strain of PT hydrogels increased dramatically with increasing water content, which is contrary to the tensile stress. The self-healing efficiency of PT hydrogels increased from ∼21 % to ∼85 % with increasing water content, revealing that water plays a vital role in the healing process along the ruptured-off area of PT hydrogels. This result also suggests that the most optimal self-healing efficiency occurred at a lower degree of swelling, indicating better self-healing activity in hydrogels with a higher cross-linking network.The healing process of PT-4 hydrogel was observed at varied healing times, with the separated two halves of the hydrogel attaching to each other after 12 h of healing. The optical microscope images clearly indicate the self-healing ability of PT-4 hydrogel. The healed PT-4 hydrogel was subsequently subjected to a stretching force to demonstrate the successful recovery. Interestingly, the self-healing process in PT-4 hydrogel occurred due to hydrogen bonds between TiO₂ and carboxylic groups (Fig. 6 ) [111].

Fig. 7 shows the photocatalytic hydrogenation mechanism, where PT-4 hydrogel facilitates the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) under light irradiation. The process involves the adsorption of 4-NP onto the photocatalyst surface, followed by the transfer of electrons and protons, leading to the hydrogenation reaction. The hydrogel matrix, combined with the photocatalytic activity of PT-4, enhances the efficiency and stability of the reaction, providing a sustainable approach for selective reduction reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing photocatalytic systems for carbon fixation
ce:section.ce:para: The global production of industrial polymers exceeds 350 million tons annually, with the majority derived from petroleum sources. This reliance on fossil fuels, coupled with the design of consumer and industrial products for short-term use and disposal, has led to the accumulation of 4600 million tons of landfill waste since 1950. The escalating urgency of climate change and environmental degradation has intensified the search for sustainable alternatives to high‑carbon-footprint materials [112].
