In this context, carbon-fixing materials have emerged as a promising class of self-healing, self-reinforcing materials capable of utilizing ambient CO₂ to extend their carbon-based backbone, offering a carbon-negative solution to traditional petroleum-derived polymers [113].

Carbon-fixing materials represent a novel approach to sustainable material design. These materials harness ambient CO₂ through photocatalytic reduction and polymerization, enabling continuous carbon mass addition that offsets microcrack formation and degradation—common issues that limit the lifespan of conventional polymers. The self-healing behavior of these materials arises from the presence of active adsorbents and photocatalysts, which facilitate CO₂ reduction and monomer polymerization at defective sites. This process not only mitigates material degradation but also contributes to carbon sequestration, making these materials an attractive alternative to traditional polymers [114].

Despite significant progress, several challenges remain in the development of carbon-fixing materials. These include low energy conversion efficiency, poor product selectivity, and the competing hydrogen evolution reaction in photocatalytic systems. Additionally, the long-term stability and scalability of these materials need to be addressed. For example, plant-based carbon-fixing systems, while innovative, are limited by the short lifespan of extracted chloroplasts and the inefficiency of biosynthesis. Replacing biological components with inorganic photocatalysts could offer a more robust and efficient solution. Recent efforts have focused on developing nanostructured and hybrid photocatalysts to improve CO₂ reduction performance. However, practical applications are hindered by low product yields (tens of μmol per gram of catalyst per hour) and the need for high-energy inputs. To overcome these limitations, future research should explore novel photocatalysts, optimize reaction conditions, and develop integrated systems that combine efficient CO₂ adsorption, photocatalytic reduction, and polymerization. Carbon-fixing materials hold significant potential for applications in the construction industry, where lightweight precursors could be shipped and subsequently grown into structural materials or self-healing protective coatings. For instance, self-healing coatings could extend the lifespan of offshore wind turbines or oil rigs, reducing maintenance costs and environmental impact. A techno-economic assessment of these materials is essential to evaluate their viability and guide commercialization efforts. In a work [115], carbon-fixing materials as compartmental composites was extend that integrate three key processes (Fig. 8): atmospheric CO₂ adsorption, photocatalytic reduction to intermediates, and polymerization of these intermediates into a self-healing composite, as illustrated in Fig. 9.

A general theoretical framework that captures the overall effect of these interconnected processes on CO₂ fixation and polymer growth rates was introduced in this work. This framework allows for the evaluation and benchmarking of any carbon-fixing material design. By using this platform, various combinations of CO₂ adsorbents, photocatalysts, and polymerization pathways can be investigated to estimate optimal growth rates and identify inherent limitations. Identifying the rate-limiting step—whether CO₂ adsorption, photocatalytic conversion, or polymerization—guides the engineering of these components, either independently or in conjunction. This framework was applied to a representative pathway for CO₂ fixation to polyoxymethylene (POM) through formaldehyde as the CO₂-derived monomer. The full chemical pathway involves CO₂ adsorption and three chemical conversions. For each step, a reaction mechanism based on experimental data, determine the optimal rate constants, and integrate these steps into a single kinetic model was developed to represent an idealized one-pot reaction system converting CO₂ to POM. By mapping polymer growth with respect to improvements in photocatalytic activity and CO₂ adsorption kinetics, a roadmap for designing and engineering carbon-fixing materials with polymer growth rates comparable to those of plants was provided.

Fig. 10 illustrates the complete chemical pathway for synthesizing a carbon-fixing poly(oxymethylene) (POM) material, which involves three key mechanistic steps. First, CO₂ is photocatalytically reduced to formaldehyde. The formaldehyde is then converted into 1,3,5-trioxane, which subsequently undergoes polymerization to form an extending POM chain. This integrated reaction network combines the photocatalytic reduction unit, trimerization unit, and polymerization unit into a single system. However, the process faces two primary bottlenecks: (i) the slow kinetics of the CO₂ reduction reaction (represented by rate constants k₁ and k₂), and (ii) the formation and polymerization of trioxane (represented by rate constant k₁₀). Addressing these challenges through chemical kinetics and reaction engineering will be critical to optimizing the overall efficiency of the system.

Therefore, carbon-fixing materials represent a transformative approach to sustainable material design, offering a carbon-negative alternative to traditional polymers. By integrating CO₂ adsorption, photocatalytic reduction, and polymerization, these materials can achieve self-healing and self-reinforcing properties while contributing to carbon sequestration. However, significant challenges remain in optimizing their performance, scalability, and economic viability. Future research should focus on developing efficient photocatalysts, improving reaction kinetics, and exploring new applications to realize the full potential of carbon-fixing materials in addressing climate change and environmental degradation.

The development of functional intelligence in building materials, particularly decorative coatings, has become a significant research focus. These coatings serve as protective barriers, shielding substrates from environmental degradation caused by UV radiation, water, NOx, microbes, aerosols, and other factors [117,118]. However, they are susceptible to micro-cracks and contamination from pollutants like dust, gases, and microorganisms, which compromise both aesthetics and structural integrity [119–122]. Research indicates that when visible pollutants cover 12 % of a clean surface, they become noticeable, significantly impacting the visual appeal of buildings [123].

To address these challenges, researchers have explored the incorporation of photocatalysts such as TiO₂ into coatings. TiO₂ is favored for its high catalytic efficiency, chemical stability, low cost, non-toxicity, and compatibility with construction materials [124–126]. However, its poor dispersion in organic solvents or aqueous systems often leads to aggregation, reducing its effectiveness in water-based coatings. To overcome this, surface modification techniques, including inorganic coating, in-situ grafting, and organic–inorganic composite assembly, have been employed to enhance TiO₂’s stability and performance [127,128].

Studies have demonstrated the potential of TiO₂-based coatings. For instance, Constantino et al. showed that increasing TiO₂ film thickness on Portland cement slurry enhanced photocatalytic activity, although carbonation affected performance [129]. Tungsten-doped TiO₂ embedded in polyurethane coatings has also proven effective in improving indoor air quality under visible light [130].

Self-healing technology represents a critical advancement in extending the lifespan of decorative coatings and maintaining their aesthetic and functional properties. Microcapsule-based self-healing coatings are particularly notable for their ease of preparation, rapid crack repair, and compatibility with various materials [131,132]. However, traditional organic-wall microcapsules often involve toxic compounds and energy-intensive synthesis processes. To address these limitations, green and non-toxic inorganic compounds, such as CaCO₃, have been proposed as alternatives for microcapsule wall materials.

Recent innovations in microcapsule technology include the development of CaCO₃-based phase-change microcapsules for waste heat recovery [133], paraffin@SiO₂ microcapsules with color modification [134], and SiO₂-TiO₂/paraffin microcapsules with high latent heat and phase transition efficiency. These advancements highlight the potential for multifunctional applications in coatings, combining self-cleaning, self-healing, and energy-efficient properties.

While hydrophobic self-cleaning and self-healing coatings have been extensively studied, coatings that combine photocatalytic self-cleaning and self-healing properties remain underexplored. Recent efforts have included antifogging hydrogel coatings [135], A-SiO₂/N-TiO₂ @ HDTMS coatings [136], and core-shell fiber/epoxy coatings with superhydrophobicity in acidic and alkaline conditions [137]. However, these coatings often exhibit limited efficacy against hydrophobic contaminants.

To address these gaps, in the other study [116] CaCO₃/TiO₂ hybrid microcapsules were synthesized using linseed oil as the core material through in-situ precipitation. These microcapsules offered dual functionality: photocatalytic self-cleaning and self-repairing. The synthesis process involves dispersing TiO₂ nanopowder in formamide, combining it with linseed oil, and stabilizing the mixture using SDBS. Calcium chloride and sodium carbonate solutions are then added sequentially to form microcapsules with a functional core-shell structure as illustrated in Fig. 10.

The microcapsules were integrated into epoxy resin matrices using the insertion method to ensure even distribution and prevent sinking. A 3:1 ratio of epoxy resin AB adhesive components (main agent and hardener) was mixed, poured into molds, and air bubbles were expelled. Partially cured resin was layered with microcapsules, followed by an additional resin layer to create a robust composite as shown in Fig. 11 . A mechanism of self cleaning and self repairing is shown in Fig.12.

The self-repairing behavior of the CaCO₃/TiO₂ hybrid microcapsules was evaluated by tracking microcrack depth changes in epoxy resin matrices with microcapsule concentrations of 0 %, 3 %, 5 %, and 7 %. The results showed that the self-healing effect was most pronounced within the first 12 h, stabilizing after 24 h, with a self-repairing rate of 71.3 % at 7 wt% microcapsule content. Higher microcapsule concentrations enhanced microcrack filling, significantly improving self-healing performance.

Mechanical property assessments revealed that increased microcapsule content improved coating hardness, achieving a grade of 3H at 7 wt%. However, adhesion decreased at higher concentrations due to reduced contact area between the paint and substrate. Impact resistance improved notably between 5 wt% and 7 wt%, attributed to enhanced intermolecular forces and the ductility provided by the rough microcapsule walls.

The microcapsules also exhibited excellent self-cleaning capabilities, with methylene blue degradation rates of 82.12 %, 87.54 %, and 93.36 % for 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% microcapsules under UV exposure for 120 min. Even after 10 reuse cycles, the degradation rate decreased by only ∼15 %, demonstrating strong reusability.

The integration of CaCO₃/TiO₂ hybrid microcapsules into coatings offers a dual-functional solution, combining self-repairing and self-cleaning properties. These materials enhance the durability, aesthetic value, and environmental performance of coatings, making them suitable for applications in construction, marine, machinery, and anti-corrosion fields. By addressing key challenges such as microcrack formation and pollutant degradation, this innovative approach paves the way for the development of advanced, multifunctional coatings that contribute to sustainable infrastructure and environmental protection. As shown in Fig. 13 , when the coating containing microcapsules experiences physical stress leading to micro-cracks, stress concentration causes the microcapsules to rupture, releasing the linseed oil core material. Upon contact with oxygen, the linolenic acid in the linseed oil undergoes a cross-linking reaction, solidifying and enabling the self-repair of the microcracks in the coating [138]. (See Fig. 14.)

Building on the advancements in self-healing microcapsule technology for coatings, similar principles have been extended to address challenges in other materials, such as concrete. Concrete structures are highly susceptible to cracking due to factors like deformation, creep, fatigue, drying shrinkage, and freeze-thaw cycles. These cracks not only weaken the structural integrity but also provide pathways for corrosive substances, accelerating deterioration. Traditional repair methods, including bacteria, super absorbent polymers, and wet carbonation, have limitations, particularly in addressing cracks caused by slow processes like concrete creep. This has led to the exploration of self-healing capsules, which contain repair agents such as cyanoacrylates, polyurethane, epoxy, silicon, hydrogels, or alkali-silica solutions. These agents are released when the capsules rupture upon crack formation. However, a significant challenge remains: capsules often require external impact to rupture, making them less effective for cracks that develop gradually. To overcome this limitation, a novel approach using photocatalytic self-healing capsules has been introduced [139]. These capsules incorporate photocatalysts, such as TiO₂, into their shells. When cracks form in the concrete, sunlight penetrates the material and activates the photocatalyst, gradually degrading the capsule shell and releasing repair agents like NaHCO₃, Ca(OH)₂, and citric acid. These agents react with water to produce CO₂ bubbles, calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), and calcium citrate (Ca₃(C₆H₅O₇)₂), which effectively fill and seal the cracks. This method leverages sunlight as a trigger for self-healing, offering a sustainable and efficient solution for concrete repair. The use of citric acid, in particular, provides economic and environmental benefits, while the generated bubbles help distribute the repair materials effectively within the cracks.

Fig. 13 depicts the self-healing mechanism: capsules filled with NaHCO₃, Ca(OH)₂, and citric acid are embedded in mortar. When cracks form, sunlight activates the capsule shell, causing it to rupture and release repair agents into the cracks.

The preparation of photocatalytic capsules involves three key steps, as illustrated in Fig. 15 . First, a mixture of NaHCO₃, Ca(OH)₂, citric acid (C₆H₈O₇), and a small amount of water is prepared and placed into spherical molds with an 8 mm diameter. The ratio of these components is carefully controlled at 200:100:200:1 to ensure cohesion and prevent premature reactions. A minimal amount of water is used to bind the mixture without triggering significant chemical reactions before encapsulation. Once shaped, the mixture is encapsulated within a shell made of polyethylene (PE) embedded with TiO₂ photocatalysts. (See Fig. 16.)

The rupture of the PE shells under sunlight is a critical step in the self-healing process. When exposed to sunlight, TiO₂ particles in the capsule shell absorb light, generating electrons and holes that interact with oxygen to form reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide anions (O₂−) and hydroxyl radicals (OH). These ROS initiate the degradation of the PE shell, eventually causing the capsules to break open and release the repair agents. This photocatalytic degradation process, while complex, is essential for enabling the self-healing mechanism in concrete.

When the repair agents (NaHCO₃, Ca(OH)₂, and citric acid) come into contact with water, they react rapidly, producing CO₂ bubbles that help push the repair materials into the cracks. A thin layer of insoluble calcium salts (CaCO₃ or Ca₃(C₆H₅O₇)₂) forms on the surface, effectively sealing the cracks. Unreacted Ca(OH)₂ mixes with water to form an adhesive lime slurry, which binds the repair materials and ensures long-term stability. Although some unreacted citric acid may remain, its corrosive effect is minimal due to the neutralization by NaHCO₃ and Ca(OH)₂, ensuring the overall durability of the repaired concrete.

This photocatalytic self-healing mechanism represents a significant advancement in concrete repair technology, complementing the development of self-healing coatings discussed earlier. Both approaches highlight the potential of combining encapsulation, photocatalytic activity, and chemical reactions to enhance material durability and sustainability across diverse applications. For instance, just as the CaCO₃/TiO₂ hybrid microcapsules in coatings demonstrate self-repairing and self-cleaning properties, the photocatalytic capsules in concrete offer a sunlight-triggered solution for crack repair. This synergy between self-healing technologies in coatings and concrete underscores the versatility of photocatalytic materials in addressing material degradation in various environments.

The experimental validation of this approach demonstrates its feasibility. When mortar cracks form and sunlight penetrate, the photocatalytic capsules break, releasing repair agents into the cracks. As water interacts with these agents, chemical reactions produce insoluble compounds like CaCO₃ and Ca₃(C₆H₅O₇)₂, which fill and seal the cracks. This method not only improves the longevity and resilience of concrete structures but also introduces a sustainable, energy-efficient repair mechanism that aligns with global efforts toward greener construction practices.

Therefore, the integration of photocatalytic self-healing capsules into concrete repair represents a promising extension of the self-healing principles explored in coatings. By leveraging sunlight as a trigger and combining photocatalytic activity with encapsulated repair agents, this approach offers a scalable and environmentally friendly solution for enhancing the durability of concrete structures. Together with the advancements in self-healing photocatalytic coatings, these innovations pave the way for a new generation of materials that can autonomously repair damage, reduce maintenance costs, and contribute to sustainable infrastructure development.

Self-cleaning films have garnered significant attention due to their ability to remove or degrade contaminants through natural forces like wind, rain, and sunlight [141,142]. These films can be categorized into superhydrophobic physical self-cleaning surfaces, inspired by the “lotus leaf effect,” and photocatalytic chemical self-cleaning surfaces, which leverage photocatalysts to degrade contaminants via electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs under light exposure [143–148]. Combining these properties, dual-functional self-cleaning films demonstrate the potential for superior performance by removing contaminants through water droplets rolling off their surface and degrading organic pollutants via photocatalysis [149–152]. Extensive research has been conducted to develop such films with stable superhydrophobicity and efficient photocatalytic activity. For instance, N-doped TiO₂ and PDMS coatings [153], ZnS nanobundle coatings [154], and PDA-assisted Ag/CdS superhydrophobic fabrics [155] have shown promising results in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. However, prolonged photocatalysis can compromise superhydrophobicity due to free radicals increasing the film's surface energy [156–158]. Moreover, challenges persist, such as chemical and mechanical vulnerability of superhydrophobicity and the low adhesion or loading of TiO₂ on the substrate, limiting the stability and efficiency of photocatalysis. TiO₂, a widely used semiconductor for self-cleaning films, offers advantages such as chemical stability, low cost, and dual functionality in enhancing surface roughness and providing photocatalytic activity [159–161]. Recent studies have focused on overcoming limitations through innovative designs, such as flower-like SiO₂@TiO₂ spheres modified with PFDTS [162] and activated carbon-supported TiO₂ composites combined with HDTMS [163]. Despite advancements, achieving self-cleaning films with robust superhydrophobicity and durable photocatalytic performance remains a challenge.

In a work [140], a dual-functional self-cleaning film with durable superhydrophobicity and photocatalytic capability is proposed using a TiO₂-modified nanopillar array. The film is fabricated through the construction of a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) nanopillar array (APDMS), the swelling absorption of anatase TiO₂ nanoparticles, and subsequent low-surface-energy modification using PFDTS (1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorodecyltrimethoxysilane). The hierarchical micro/nanostructure and robust adhesion between TiO₂ and the APDMS substrate enhance the film's durability and photocatalytic efficiency. The schematic of the fabrication process is shown in Fig. 15.

The F-TiO₂/APDMS film exhibits excellent water repellency, with various liquids (e.g., water, coffee, milk, tea, acidic, and alkaline droplets) forming stable spherical shapes on the surface. When immersed in water, the rough structure traps air, producing a mirror-like surface. The film's low sliding angle (2°) ensures that contaminants are easily removed by rolling water droplets. The film maintains superhydrophobicity (water contact angles [WCAs] above 155°) after immersion in various solvents (e.g., ethanol, hexane, and DMF) for 72 h. It also resists extreme pH conditions (HCl at pH 1 and NaOH at pH 13) and remains superhydrophobic (WCAs ∼151°) even after prolonged exposure. Additionally, the film withstands temperature extremes (−5 °C to 100 °C) for 72 h without significant loss of water repellency. Mechanical tests confirm the film's durability under stretching, water impact, and sand abrasion. The film endures stretching strains up to 160 %, with WCAs remaining above 153° after 500 cycles. It resists water jet impacts (15 mL/s at 10 cm for 10 min) without liquid penetration, maintaining a WCA of 158°. Similarly, after sand abrasion, the film retains its superhydrophobicity (WCA ∼153°), with the surface morphology largely unchanged.

The F-TiO₂/APDMS film achieves over 98 % degradation efficiency of organic pollutants under UV exposure, attributed to the photocatalytic activity of TiO₂ nanoparticles. Furthermore, the film can repair its superhydrophobicity when damaged during extended UV irradiation, ensuring consistent performance for both physical and chemical self-cleaning. The addition of anatase-typed TiO₂ nanoparticles provided the F-TiO₂/APDMS film with excellent photocatalytic activity, enhancing its chemical self-cleaning ability. Contaminants like methylene blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB) were effectively degraded under UV irradiation, with the film regaining its clean appearance within minutes. Even after repeated cycles of dye contamination and UV treatment, the film maintained its hierarchical roughness and elemental composition (Fig. 17 ). The TiO₂ nanoparticles dispersed in THF showed superior photocatalytic efficiency compared to those in ethanol.

The F-TiO₂/APDMS film combines durable superhydrophobicity with efficient photocatalytic capability, demonstrating resistance to chemical, physical, and environmental damages. This performance is ascribed to the synergistic effect of the hierarchical micro/nanostructure and chemically grafted PFDTS. These findings highlight the potential of the dual-functional film for advanced self-cleaning applications. In recent years, the development of superhydrophobic (SH) surfaces with self-cleaning and photocatalytic properties has gained significant attention due to their potential for diverse industrial and environmental applications. In a work introduces a novel method for fabricating multifunctional and environmentally durable SH surfaces through a straightforward one-step reaction of octadecyl isocyanate (ODI) with TiO₂ particles was developed [164]. The resulting films exhibit SH properties due to the synergistic combination of hydrophobic long alkyl chains and a hierarchical crystalline structure. These films can be easily applied via spray or blade coating on various substrates, including cloth, paper, and metal, demonstrating excellent performance in both air and oil environments. The SH properties of the coatings remain stable for at least six months under ambient conditions and can recover their SH functionality after exposure to organic pollutants through UV or sunlight illumination. When exposed to water, the SH properties can self-heal within hours at ambient temperature or within minutes under heating, driven by the self-assembly of long alkyl chains back to their original configuration. This self-healing behavior is further supported by the strong covalent bonds between the alkyl chains and TiO₂, as well as hydrogen bonds between adjacent alkyl chains, which enhance the durability and longevity of the SH films.

The study also explores the potential of self-healing SH coatings to maintain long-term resistance against water penetration and mechanical abrasion. Previous research has demonstrated the use of temperature- or moisture-triggered agents to restore SH properties, as well as abrasion-induced roughness regeneration. In this work, the grafting of octadecyl chains from ODI onto TiO₂ nanoparticles in a simple one-step reaction leads to the formation of SH surfaces through hierarchical microstructure development driven by spontaneous crystallization. The incorporation of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) further enhances the mechanical robustness and longevity of the coating due to its cross-linked structure. This SH coating, fabricated using straightforward techniques, exhibits impressive self-cleaning properties and offers additional desirable features such as enhanced buoyancy, adjustable transparency, and resistance to oil contamination, making it suitable for a wide range of industrial applications.

In practical applications, the SH coating demonstrates effective water repellency and liquid resistance when deposited on various substrates, as shown in Fig. 18A. The self-cleaning properties were evaluated by applying dirt or methylene blue (MB) dye to the SH-coated glass surface, where water droplets efficiently removed the contaminants upon rolling off. Furthermore, the SH coating maintains its properties even when exposed to oil, as demonstrated in Fig. 18B. When immersed in oil, water droplets on the coated surface exhibited high water contact angles (WCAs), indicating that the SH properties were not compromised by oil exposure. Dirt contamination tests performed at the oil-water-air interface showed that the coating could effectively remove soil contaminants with water droplets, both in air and submerged in oil. Additionally, the SH coating significantly increased the buoyancy of materials, as evidenced by a coated glass slide floating and supporting 900 times its weight before submerging, a result attributed to the interaction between the film's hierarchical structure and oil.

The photocatalytic properties of the coating were verified through the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under UV light, with the TiO₂ coating significantly breaking down MB molecules over time. Remarkably, the SH coating not only retained its properties after exposure to UV and sunlight for extended periods (up to six months) but also demonstrated the ability to self-heal its SH properties after mechanical damage or contamination. When exposed to oil-based contaminants, the photocatalytic degradation of the oil restored the coating's original SH properties. The durability of the coating was further tested under harsh conditions, including exposure to acidic and alkaline solutions, as well as mechanical abrasion. The coating showed impressive stability against weak acids, and after exposure to rain-like water impact, it exhibited self-healing via thermal assistance. The mechanical robustness of the coating was further enhanced by the use of adhesive layers beneath the SH film, which helped retain SH properties even after multiple abrasion cycles.

Overall, this multifunctional SH coating, with its photocatalytic, self-healing, and oil-repellent properties, holds great potential for a wide range of industrial applications. Its ease of fabrication, durability, and environmental resistance make it a robust solution for long-term use in self-cleaning surfaces, oil/water separation, and protective coatings, offering significant benefits for both commercial and industrial sectors.

Magnesium alloys are widely utilized in industries such as aviation, automotive, and electronics due to their low density, high specific strength, and excellent electromagnetic shielding properties [165,166]. Additionally, their degradability, mechanical properties comparable to bone, and biocompatibility have made them attractive for medical applications. However, their high chemical reactivity and susceptibility to rapid corrosion limit their widespread use. To address this, various strategies have been employed, including surface treatments like micro-arc oxidation (MAO), which creates ceramic layers with porous structures that significantly enhance corrosion resistance [167–170].

To further enhance the functionalities of magnesium alloys, such as self-warning and self-repairing capabilities, functionalized MAO coatings have been developed. Two principal approaches are generally employed: (1) adding functional additives directly into the electrolyte to facilitate in-situ formation of functionalized MAO coatings [171,172], and (2) preparing a functional thin film on the surface of the MAO layer to create a composite coating [173]. These methods aim to expand the utility of MAO coatings, enabling them to respond to environmental changes or self-repair, thereby improving their performance and applicability across various fields.

Polydopamine (PDA) has emerged as a promising material for functional coatings due to its multi-molecular structure, which includes catechols and primary amines. These functional groups provide PDA with remarkable modifiability and adhesion properties, making it an attractive candidate for surface modification [174]. The preparation of MAO-PDA composite coatings on magnesium alloys leverages the advantages of both the strengthening layer and the modified layer. However, traditional methods like impregnation require long deposition times and may degrade the MAO layer through exposure to corrosive ions, weakening its corrosion resistance [175].

To address these limitations, a novel method for preparing MAO-PDA composite coatings was introduced [176]. This method involves adding K₂TiF₆ to the electrolyte to produce an MAO coating with smaller pores, followed by PDA film deposition under ultraviolet (UV) light. The resulting MAO-PDA composite coatings exhibit excellent corrosion resistance and facilitate surface modification. UV irradiation promotes the deposition of PDA, as evidenced by the darker color and higher C content in UV-treated samples compared to non-UV-treated ones.

The macroscopic morphology of the MAO-PDA samples (Fig. 19a) shows a dark brown color on the surfaces, attributed to the deposition of PDA. Samples under UV irradiation exhibit a darker color compared to those without UV exposure, likely due to the promotion of dopamine polymerization by UV light, leading to more PDA deposition. The 3D surface morphology (Fig. 19b) reveals that surface roughness does not significantly change with or without UV conditions. Samples prepared with K₂TiF₆ exhibit fewer pores and better surface integrity compared to those without, attributed to the hydrolysis of K₂TiF₆ in the electrolyte, forming TiO₂ particles that fill the pores in the MAO film [177]. Therefore, this study demonstrates the effective integration of photocatalytic principles into the preparation of MAO-PDA coatings on magnesium alloy surfaces. The use of UV light significantly enhances the deposition of PDA, leading to improved corrosion resistance and coating integrity. The incorporation of K₂TiF₆ into the MAO layer plays a critical role in reducing pore size and strengthening the coating, providing a more robust foundation for potential multifunctional modifications. The findings also suggest that the photocatalytic strategy applied here could be extended to other alloys, such as titanium-based alloys, to further improve the performance and longevity of coatings in various applications.

Table 1 summarizes recent advancements in photocatalytic self-healing materials, highlighting the integration of self-healing properties with photocatalytic functionality across various applications. Many studies, such as those on TiO2-based films and CaCO3/TiO2 hybrid microcapsules, demonstrate successful self-repair and photocatalytic degradation, making these materials suitable for self-cleaning, protective coatings, and environmental remediation.

Despite the promising results, some challenges remain, including the long-term durability of self-healing abilities, potential degradation of photocatalytic performance over time, and difficulties in scaling these systems for industrial use. These limitations underscore the need for further research to enhance the stability, efficiency, and commercial viability of self-healing photocatalysts.
The following section explores potential strategies for overcoming these challenges and advancing the practical applications of these materials.

The development of self-repairing photocatalytic systems marks a transformative shift in photocatalysis, addressing long-standing challenges such as catalyst deactivation, material fatigue, and performance decay. By mimicking nature's mechanisms, particularly the self-healing systems found in photosynthetic organisms, artificial photocatalysts that can repair themselves hold great promise for a range of applications, from solar fuel production to environmental remediation. The integration of dynamic self-assembly, molecular recognition, and autonomous healing mechanisms enables photocatalysts to recover from damage and maintain high levels of efficiency even under extreme conditions.

The promise of self-repairing photocatalysts is particularly compelling in the context of sustainable energy production. For example, solar fuel production—especially hydrogen generation via water splitting—relies on photocatalysts that are often exposed to harsh oxidative environments, leading to degradation over time. By incorporating self-repair capabilities, these materials could potentially extend their operational lifespan, reduce the need for frequent catalyst replacement, and lower the overall costs of renewable energy production. Furthermore, self-healing photocatalysts are poised to revolutionize environmental cleanup, as they can continuously degrade pollutants and pollutants in water and air, thus contributing to long-term environmental sustainability.

Key advancements in the field include the development of advanced materials that are able to self-repair at the molecular or atomic level. For instance, recent studies have explored bio-inspired approaches where photocatalysts are engineered to rebuild their active sites after they undergo photocorrosion or surface fouling. Surface reconstruction and regeneration strategies have also been shown to significantly enhance the stability of photocatalysts. These repair mechanisms, combined with high-performance materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), carbon-based nanomaterials, and hybrid semiconductor composites, hold great potential for the creation of highly durable photocatalysts.

Despite these advances, the path to commercialization is not without challenges. One of the major obstacles is scaling up self-repairing photocatalytic systems from laboratory demonstrations to industrial applications. The ability to maintain the self-repair capabilities of these systems under real-world conditions—such as prolonged exposure to light, heat, and environmental pollutants—requires further refinement of the underlying materials and mechanisms. For example, achieving a balance between the repair kinetics and the thermodynamics of the repair process is crucial to ensure that the process is both efficient and energetically favorable.

The integration of machine learning and artificial intelligence (AI) into the design and optimization of self-repairing photocatalysts will likely play a critical role in overcoming these challenges. AI can aid in the design of new materials, predict degradation patterns, and optimize the self-repair processes to maximize performance. Additionally, AI-powered systems can facilitate real-time monitoring and adjustment of photocatalytic systems, further enhancing their efficiency and longevity.

Looking toward the future, several key areas of development can significantly enhance the potential of self-repairing photocatalysts:
1.
New Materials and Multi-Functional Approaches: The design of hybrid photocatalysts that combine multiple functionalities, such as light absorption, charge transport, and self-repair, will be crucial. Researchers are exploring novel materials, such as perovskite semiconductors, graphene, and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), that exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity and can be engineered for self-healing. Multi-functional composites that integrate photocatalysts with sensors or electrochemical elements could lead to intelligent systems capable of detecting damage and autonomously initiating repair processes.
2.
Hybrid and Multifunctional Systems: Combining self-repairing photocatalysts with other advanced technologies, such as solar cells, supercapacitors, or batteries, could lead to multifunctional systems that can operate in parallel, further enhancing their efficiency. For example, integrating a self-repairing photocatalyst with energy storage systems could lead to systems capable of generating and storing energy simultaneously, thus improving the overall energy efficiency.
3.
Sustainability and Environmental Impact: As the demand for green technologies grows, the environmental footprint of photocatalytic systems must also be considered. Future research will focus on ensuring that self-repairing photocatalysts are not only efficient and cost-effective but also environmentally friendly. This includes investigating the life cycle of these materials, evaluating their recyclability, and exploring sustainable synthesis methods.
4.
Commercialization and Large-Scale Deployment: A major area of future research will be the scaling-up of self-repairing photocatalysts for industrial applications. This includes developing cost-effective manufacturing processes and ensuring the reliability and durability of these systems in real-world settings. For instance, photocatalytic systems used in water treatment and air purification need to maintain their self-repair capabilities while operating under continuous stress.
5.
Regulatory and Economic Factors: The successful deployment of self-repairing photocatalysts will require addressing regulatory concerns related to safety, material standards, and environmental impact. Economic assessments will also be crucial in determining the cost-effectiveness of these technologies compared to traditional alternatives. The economic viability of self-repairing photocatalytic systems will depend on balancing the initial investment in research and development with the long-term savings associated with increased system lifespan and reduced maintenance costs.
In conclusion, self-repairing photocatalysts represent a revolutionary step toward sustainable and efficient photocatalytic systems. While significant progress has been made in material design, repair mechanisms, and characterization techniques, overcoming challenges related to scalability, stability, and cost-effectiveness will be key to their widespread adoption. Continued interdisciplinary research, coupled with advances in AI and machine learning, will drive the future development of self-repairing photocatalysts and unlock their full potential in applications ranging from renewable energy production to environmental remediation.
Roya Mohammadzadeh Kakhki: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
